content: Wait‚Ä¶ a web page can run AI? Yep. Thanks to TensorFlow.js, your browser isn‚Äôt just for reading articles, watching videos, or endlessly scrolling social media. It can load neural networks, process webcam frames, recognize speech, and make predictions locally in real time‚Äî feeling a bit like giving your browser a brain. But how does it actually pull this off? Let‚Äôs take a look under the hood.
title: A Friendly Dive into How TensorFlow.js Actually Works

content: 5 Four big ideas, one clever libraryEverything TensorFlow.js does hangs off four core concepts. Get these, and the rest of the architecture snaps into focus.
title: A Friendly Dive into How TensorFlow.js Actually Works

content: 5.1 Idea 1 ‚Äî Tensors: how TensorFlow.js represents dataThink of tensors as the universal language of TensorFlow.js. A number is a tensor. A list is a tensor. An image? A tensor with more dimensions. Three things make them special: They‚Äôre immutable ‚Äî every operation creates a new tensor rather than modifying the old one. They can live in GPU memory, which is how the fast backends get their speed. And they scale in dimensions, from a single value all the way up to batches of images. Same idea, more dimensions: from a single row of numbers to a whole batch of images.
title: A Friendly Dive into How TensorFlow.js Actually Works

content: 5.2 Idea 2 ‚Äî APIs: how TensorFlow.js defines the computationHere‚Äôs the genuinely clever part. When your code says ‚Äúmultiply these matrices,‚Äù TensorFlow.js doesn‚Äôt have one way to do that ‚Äî it has five. A backend selector inspects the environment and routes every operation to the fastest engine available, falling back gracefully when fancy hardware isn‚Äôt around. Write the model once. The selector picks the engine at runtime. Backend Think of it as Best for WebGPU Modern GPU compute, no disguises The fastest option, where supported WebGL A GPU smuggled in via graphics shaders Most browsers today WASM A supercharged CPU Small models, wide compatibility CPU The universal fallback Always works Node.js Native TensorFlow, no browser needed Servers
title: A Friendly Dive into How TensorFlow.js Actually Works

content: 5.3 Idea 3 ‚Äî Backends: where the computation runsHere‚Äôs the rite of passage every TensorFlow.js developer goes through: your demo runs beautifully for two minutes, then your GPU memory quietly fills up and the tab keels over. Why? Because tensors aren‚Äôt automatically garbage collected. Many of them live in GPU memory, which JavaScript‚Äôs garbage collector can‚Äôt see. Every intermediate tensor you create sticks around until you explicitly let it go. The fix is delightfully simple: wrap temporary work in tf.tidy(), and it sweeps up every intermediate tensor when the function returns. For tensors you manage by hand, call dispose() when you‚Äôre done. javascript // Without tidy: leaks `a` and `b` into GPU memory // With tidy: intermediates are cleaned up automatically const result = tf.tidy(() => { const a = tf.tensor([1, 2, 3]); const b = a.square(); // intermediate ‚Äî swept up return b.sum(); // survives the tidy }); Rule of thumb: if you created a tensor and didn‚Äôt return it, someone has to clean it up ‚Äî and that someone is tf.tidy(), or it‚Äôs you.
title: A Friendly Dive into How TensorFlow.js Actually Works

content: 5.4 Idea 4 ‚Äî Lifecycle: load, train, manage, save, and deployYou‚Äôve got two paths to a working model. Build and train it entirely in JavaScript, or import a trained TensorFlow or Keras model from the Python world. Either way, after conversion a deployed model is refreshingly boring: a model.json file describing the architecture, plus binary weight shards. Static files. Host them anywhere you‚Äôd host an image. Train anywhere ‚Üí convert once ‚Üí serve as static files ‚Üí predict in the tab.
title: A Friendly Dive into How TensorFlow.js Actually Works

content: 6 Takeaway: how it works under the hoodTensorFlow.js isn‚Äôt just ‚ÄúTensorFlow in JavaScript.‚Äù It‚Äôs a system where each layer has a clear responsibility. Tensors represent data, the APIs describe computations, backends execute those computations on available hardware, and lifecycle tools manage models from training to deployment. Together, these layers let you write a few lines of JavaScript while TensorFlow.js handles the messy details of memory management, hardware acceleration, and model execution behind the scenes.
title: A Friendly Dive into How TensorFlow.js Actually Works

content: 7 Easter egg: where Teachable Machine fitsTeachable Machine is one of those tools. It hides almost all of TensorFlow.js behind a friendly interface: you collect examples, label classes, and click Train. Behind those buttons, TensorFlow.js converts your data into tensors, fine-tunes a pre-trained model, executes the computation using the browser‚Äôs available backend, and exports a model.json file together with its weights. That‚Äôs how the magic works! Next, let‚Äôs see another black box behind TM - transform learning! Cheers!
title: A Friendly Dive into How TensorFlow.js Actually Works

content: 8 Primary sources Smilkov, Thorat, Assogba, et al. (2019). TensorFlow.js: Machine Learning for the Web and Beyond (arXiv:1901.05350) ‚Äî The official TensorFlow.js paper introducing its architecture, tensors, APIs, and execution backends. TensorFlow.js Official Documentation ¬∑ GitHub Repository ‚Äî Official documentation, source code, and backend implementations. TensorFlow.js Guide: Model Conversion ‚Äî tensorflowjs_converter, weight sharding, Grappler optimization, and model loading. content: 1 The Secret Behind a Folded PizzaPicture this: today is pizza night! You grab a fresh, cheesy slice of pizza. It‚Äôs hot, it‚Äôs gooey‚Ä¶ and it immediately flops over like it‚Äôs given up on life. So, what do you do? You pull the classic move ‚Äî you fold the slice. Just a quick bend along the crust toward the center, and magically, the floppy slice becomes firm and easy to hold! But here‚Äôs the wild part ‚Äî that little fold isn‚Äôt just a clever eating hack. It also hides a beautiful piece of geometry ‚Äî Gaussian curvature.
title: From a Slice of Pizza to Gauss's ‚ÄúRemarkable Theorem‚Äù--- Exploring Gaussian Curvature

content: 2 Gaussian CurvaturePicture that slice of pizza in your hand. Before you fold it, it‚Äôs just a flat piece of dough ‚Äî smooth, even, and perfectly level. Every point on it is flat, meaning its curvature is zero. In mathematics, we describe how a surface bends by looking at two special directions at any point: the maximum value of curvature in one direction ‚Äî that‚Äôs $k_1$. the minimum value of curvature in the other direction ‚Äî that‚Äôs $k_2$. Multiply those two numbers, and you get the Gaussian curvature: K=k1‚ãÖk2.K = k_1 \cdot k_2.K=k1‚Äã‚ãÖk2‚Äã.And just like that, different surfaces reveal their own ‚Äúbending personalities‚Äù. Before you fold the pizza, it‚Äôs a flat surface ‚Äî which means K=k1√ók2=0√ó0=0.K = k_1 \times k_2 = 0 \times 0 = 0.K=k1‚Äã√ók2‚Äã=0√ó0=0.And here‚Äôs the beautiful part: according to Gauss‚Äôs Remarkable Theorem, The Gaussian curvature of a surface does not change if you bend the surface without stretching it. In other words ‚Äî folding the pizza doesn‚Äôt change its Gaussian curvature. That is, even after the fold, it still belongs to the geometric world of plane.
title: From a Slice of Pizza to Gauss's ‚ÄúRemarkable Theorem‚Äù--- Exploring Gaussian Curvature

content: 3 Why Does the Pizza Hold Up?Why does a folded pizza suddenly become strong enough to hold all that gooey cheese and sauce? From a mathematical point of view, the pizza‚Äôs Gaussian curvature $K=0 $, both before and after you fold it. Then when you fold the slice along one direction, turning it into a neat little ‚ÄúV‚Äù shape, something interesting happens: In the direction across the fold, the pizza bends, which means $k_1 \neq 0$. Hence, along the fold curve itself, it must be $k_2 = 0$, meaning, that fold curve is a straight line, staying perfectly flat. In other words, folding the slice greatly increases its stiffness along that fold line. Once folded, the pizza can‚Äôt bend easily in that direction‚Äî that‚Äôs why it suddenly gains strength, feels firm, steady, and ready to handle all that cheesy weight.
title: From a Slice of Pizza to Gauss's ‚ÄúRemarkable Theorem‚Äù--- Exploring Gaussian Curvature

content: 4 Developable vs. Non-developable SurfacesIn math, if a smooth surface has Gaussian curvature K = 0 everywhere, we call it a developable surface. In simple terms, it‚Äôs the kind of surface you could flatten perfectly onto a sheet of paper without stretching or tearing. Think of: a folded pizza slice, a roll of toilet paper, or an ice-cream cone By contrast, if a surface has places where its Gaussian curvature isn‚Äôt zero ($K \neq 0$), it‚Äôs called a non-developable surface. That means it refuses to lie flat ‚Äî you can‚Äôt turn it into a plane without stretching, squeezing, or ripping it apart, no matter how hard you try. Orange peel: it curves in every direction ($K > 0$), which is why it can never lie flat. Potato chip: one side curves up, the other curves down ($K < 0$); try to flatten it, and it‚Äôll snap. This also explains why a perfectly distortion-free world map is impossible. The Earth‚Äôs surface is curved ‚Äî it has positive Gaussian curvature ‚Äî while paper has zero curvature. And by Gauss‚Äôs Remarkable Theorem, bending without stretching doesn‚Äôt change curvature. So when we try to project our round planet onto a flat map, something has to give ‚Äî area, shape, or distance will always end up distorted. Not a design flaw ‚Äî just mathematics doing its thing!
title: From a Slice of Pizza to Gauss's ‚ÄúRemarkable Theorem‚Äù--- Exploring Gaussian Curvature

content: 5 From Pizza to the CosmosGaussian curvature isn‚Äôt just a neat geometric curiosity ‚Äî it‚Äôs one of the cornerstones of modern physicsÔºÅ A century after Gauss, Albert Einstein took this elegant idea and used it to build General Relativity. In Einstein‚Äôs universe, gravity isn‚Äôt some invisible force tugging at planets and stars ‚Äî it‚Äôs the curvature of spacetime itself.
title: From a Slice of Pizza to Gauss's ‚ÄúRemarkable Theorem‚Äù--- Exploring Gaussian Curvature

content: 6 Reference https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Theorema_Egregium https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/General_relativity https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Developable_surface
title: From a Slice of Pizza to Gauss's ‚ÄúRemarkable Theorem‚Äù--- Exploring Gaussian Curvature

content: 1 Story Time TwistThe other night, I was reading The Tortoise and the Hare with my kid. You know the ending ‚Äî the rabbit takes a nap, the tortoise keeps going, and‚Ä¶ yep, the tortoise wins. Classic. But when we finished the story ‚Äî yeah, you caught me ‚Äî I just couldn‚Äôt resist adding a twist: " What if the very next day‚Ä¶ they race again?" This time, the rabbit wants to look ‚Äúfair.‚Äù He smirks and says: ‚ÄúTortoise, you‚Äôre way too slow. To make things exciting, I‚Äôll give you a 100-meter head start!‚Äù The race begins. Whoooosh! The rabbit blasts off and quickly covers that 100 meters. But when he hits the tortoise‚Äôs starting line ‚Äî guess what? the tortoise has crawled 10 meters farther. The rabbit keeps running fast and quickly caught up the 10 meters ‚Ä¶ only to see the tortoise has moved ahead by 1 more meter. The rabbit tries again. He runs that 1 meter in almost no time‚Ä¶ but the tortoise has gone 0.1 meter farther‚Ä¶ And so it goes ‚Äî every time the rabbit gets close, the tortoise is just a little farther ahead! At this point, the kid‚Äôs eyes went wide: ‚ÄúWait, what? Does that mean the rabbit can never catch the tortoise?‚Äù Is that possible? Or is there a deeper trick hiding here?
title: The Tortoise and The Hare --- Zeno Phenomenon (2)

content: 2 Intuitive ExplanationI gave a little mysterious smile, ‚ÄúHey, remember when you raced with your friend Andy last week? He dashed ahead quite a long way while you weren‚Äôt looking. And‚Ä¶ what happened then?‚Äù My kid jumped in right away, full of confidence: ‚ÄúI caught him! Because I was much faster ‚Äî and I even passed him!‚Äù Then he slapped his forehead like he‚Äôd just solved the biggest puzzle: ‚ÄúOHHHH‚Äîso the rabbit can catch the tortoise after all!‚Äù I nodded, with a gentle grin: " Yep! The faster one always catches the slower one in an amount of time‚Ä¶ even if it feels like you‚Äôre chasing again, and again, and again."
title: The Tortoise and The Hare --- Zeno Phenomenon (2)

content: 3 The ‚ÄúTrap‚Äù of Zeno PhenomenonIn the story, every time the rabbit gets to where the tortoise was, the tortoise has already moved a tiny bit ahead. It feels like the rabbit is always just a little too late, and can never catch up! This is the very ‚Äôtrap‚Äô of Zeno‚Äôs paradox: mixing up infinitely many chasing steps with an infinitely long amount of time. Up next, let‚Äôs bring in the idea of limits to reveal the surprise: even with infinitely many steps, the total time is actually finite! Alight, fun time for us big kids ‚Äì ready, go!
title: The Tortoise and The Hare --- Zeno Phenomenon (2)

content: 4 Math Explanation
title: The Tortoise and The Hare --- Zeno Phenomenon (2)

content: 4.1 SetupTortoise has a head start distance $D$. Tortoise speed: $v_T$. Rabbit speed: $v_A$, with $v_A > v_T$. We break the chase into infinitely many segments: each time the rabbit runs to where the tortoise was, the tortoise moves further.
title: The Tortoise and The Hare --- Zeno Phenomenon (2)

content: 4.2 Distances / gapsLet $d_1 = D.$ Time taken by Achilles to cover $d_1$ is t1=d1vA=DvA.t_1 = \frac{d_1}{v_A} = \frac{D}{v_A}.t1‚Äã=vA‚Äãd1‚Äã‚Äã=vA‚ÄãD‚Äã. During that time, tortoise advances: Œî1=t1‚ãÖvT=DvA‚ãÖvT.\Delta_1 = t_1 \cdot v_T = \frac{D}{v_A} \cdot v_T.Œî1‚Äã=t1‚Äã‚ãÖvT‚Äã=vA‚ÄãD‚Äã‚ãÖvT‚Äã. Thus the remaining gap this time becomes d2=Œî1.d_2 = \Delta_1 .d2‚Äã=Œî1‚Äã. By the same logic, t2=d2vA=DvA‚ãÖvTvA. t_2 = \frac{d_2}{v_A} = \frac{D}{v_A} \cdot \frac{v_T}{v_A}.t2‚Äã=vA‚Äãd2‚Äã‚Äã=vA‚ÄãD‚Äã‚ãÖvA‚ÄãvT‚Äã‚Äã. Then the tortoise advances again, gap shrinks, etc. We get the general term tn=dnvA=DvA(vTvA)‚Äân‚àí1. \quad t_n = \frac{d_n}{v_A} = \frac{D}{v_A} \left(\frac{v_T}{v_A}\right)^{\,n-1}.tn‚Äã=vA‚Äãdn‚Äã‚Äã=vA‚ÄãD‚Äã(vA‚ÄãvT‚Äã‚Äã)n‚àí1.
title: The Tortoise and The Hare --- Zeno Phenomenon (2)

content: 4.3 Time sum (infinite series)So the total time Achilles needs to catch the tortoise is T=‚àën=1‚àûtn=DvA‚àën=0‚àû(vTvA)n. T = \sum_{n=1}^\infty t_n = \frac{D}{v_A} \sum_{n=0}^\infty \left(\frac{v_T}{v_A}\right)^n.T=n=1‚àë‚àû‚Äãtn‚Äã=vA‚ÄãD‚Äãn=0‚àë‚àû‚Äã(vA‚ÄãvT‚Äã‚Äã)n.Since $\tfrac{v_T}{v_A} < 1$,the series converges (geometric series): ‚àën=0‚àû(vTvA)n=11‚àívTvA=vAvA‚àívT.\sum_{n=0}^\infty \left(\frac{v_T}{v_A}\right)^n = \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{v_T}{v_A}} = \frac{v_A}{v_A - v_T}.n=0‚àë‚àû‚Äã(vA‚ÄãvT‚Äã‚Äã)n=1‚àívA‚ÄãvT‚Äã‚Äã1‚Äã=vA‚Äã‚àívT‚ÄãvA‚Äã‚Äã. Hence T=DvA‚ãÖvAvA‚àívT=DvA‚àívT.T = \frac{D}{v_A} \cdot \frac{v_A}{v_A - v_T} = \frac{D}{v_A - v_T}.T=vA‚ÄãD‚Äã‚ãÖvA‚Äã‚àívT‚ÄãvA‚Äã‚Äã=vA‚Äã‚àívT‚ÄãD‚Äã.That is a finite positive time. After that time $T$, the rabbit catches up.
title: The Tortoise and The Hare --- Zeno Phenomenon (2)

content: 5 ClosingJust like the race between the Tortoise and the Hare, or that treasure hunt we talked about earlier with steady speed, the idea of infinitely many events fitting into a limited time is what we call a Zeno behavior. Think of dropping a little ball: Boing! it bounces high the first time, then lower, then even lower‚Ä¶ The trick to handling problems like these is: instead of getting stuck on the thought of ‚Äòforever‚Äô, we assume a definite stopping time, and then calculate the final result after all those infinitely tiny bounces.‚Äù Bam! >_<
title: The Tortoise and The Hare --- Zeno Phenomenon (2)

The second day, he walks half of what‚Äôs left, which is $1/4$. The third day, he goes half of the remainder, which is $1/8$. And it keeps going, and going, and going‚Ä¶‚Äù So, will Leo ever reach the treasure? Well, here‚Äôs the twist: the answer is‚Ä¶ NOPE! Why? Let‚Äôs dive in and find out!‚Äù

content: 1 Story Time Twist:A few days ago, while reading Pedro‚Äôs Pirate Treasure to my kid, I made up a new puzzle story: Imagine you are the little pirate, Leo. One day, you spot a brand-new mark on the treasure map! Excited, you set off on your adventure. But oh no‚Äîthe map has a strange spell written on it: ‚ÄòTo reach the treasure, you can only walk half of the distance left each time. No faster, no slower.‚Äô So, the first time, you walk half of the distance, that is $1/2$. The second time, you walk half of what‚Äôs left, which is $1/4$. The third time, you go half of the remainder, which is $1/8$. And it keeps going, and going, and going‚Ä¶" The kid shouted: ‚ÄúOh no! This will never end! Does that mean I can never get there?‚Äù I smiled and said: ‚ÄúNot true! Imagine this: if it takes you one whole day to walk the first half, then the rest‚Äîno matter how many tiny steps it‚Äôs split into‚Äîonly takes one more day. So, in just two days, boom‚Äîyou‚Äôve got the treasure!‚Äù The kid: ‚ÄúOHHHH, haha, I get it!‚Äù Now it‚Äôs fun time for us big kids! @_@ Let‚Äôs see the math explanation.
title: Treasure Hunting ---Zeno Phenomenon (1)

content: 2 Math Explanation:The trick isn‚Äôt only about ‚ÄúCan you get there?‚Äù ‚Äî it‚Äôs really about ‚ÄúCan you make it in a limited amount of time?‚Äù After all, nobody in real life keeps walking forever! That‚Äôs why looking at the time makes more sense than just adding up the distance.
title: Treasure Hunting ---Zeno Phenomenon (1)

content: 2.1 Time Calculation:Let the total distance from $A$ to $B$ be $D$. Assume motion is at constant speed $v$. We may slice the journey into infinitely many segments: first cover $D/2$, then $D/4$, then $D/8$ ‚Ä¶ Let the distance of the $n-th$ segment be: dn=D2n,n=1,2,3,‚Ä¶d_n = \frac{D}{2^n}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3,\dotsdn‚Äã=2nD‚Äã,n=1,2,3,‚Ä¶The total time after n segments is Tn=‚àëk=1ntk=1v‚àëk=1ndk=1v‚àëk=1nD2k=1vSn. T_n = \sum_{k=1}^n t_k = \frac{1}{v} \sum_{k=1}^n d_k = \frac{1}{v} \sum_{k=1}^n \frac{D}{2^k} =\frac{1}{v} S_n. Tn‚Äã=k=1‚àën‚Äãtk‚Äã=v1‚Äãk=1‚àën‚Äãdk‚Äã=v1‚Äãk=1‚àën‚Äã2kD‚Äã=v1‚ÄãSn‚Äã. Thus lim‚Å°n‚Üí‚àûTn=1vlim‚Å°n‚Üí‚àûSn=Dvlim‚Å°n‚Üí‚àû‚àëk=1n12k=Dv.\lim_{n\to\infty} T_n = \frac{1}{v} \lim_{n\to\infty} S_n = \frac{D}{v}\lim_{n\to\infty} \sum_{k=1}^n \frac{1}{2^k} = \frac{D}{v}.n‚Üí‚àûlim‚ÄãTn‚Äã=v1‚Äãn‚Üí‚àûlim‚ÄãSn‚Äã=vD‚Äãn‚Üí‚àûlim‚Äãk=1‚àën‚Äã2k1‚Äã=vD‚Äã.Hence even though you ‚Äúdivide into infinitely many times or steps,‚Äù the total time is finite, namely $D/v$. You arrive at B at time $T = D/v$.
title: Treasure Hunting ---Zeno Phenomenon (1)

content: 2.2 TipsThus the paradox is resolved: infinite subdivision does not force infinite time, because each successive distance moved forward gets smaller and smaller, making the time contributions shrink to zero. Bingo! ^_^
title: Treasure Hunting ---Zeno Phenomenon (1)

content: 2.1 Idea 1:The curve $C$ consists of $C_1 :y=3, 2\leq x \leq -2$,$C_2 :x=-2, 3\leq y \leq -3$, $C_3 :y=3, -2\leq x \leq 2$,$C_4 :x=2, -3\leq y \leq 3$, and the diagram is given as below: Hence, $$\begin{aligned}& \quad \int_C\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}dy-\frac{y}{x^2+y^2}dx \\& =\int_{C_1+C_2+C_3+C_4}\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}dy-\frac{y}{x^2+y^2}dx \\& =\int_{C_1}\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}dy-\frac{y}{x^2+y^2}dx + \int_{C_2}\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}dy-\frac{y}{x^2+y^2}dx \\& \quad + \int_{C_3}\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}dy-\frac{y}{x^2+y^2}dx + \int_{C_4}\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}dy-\frac{y}{x^2+y^2}dx \\& =\int_{2}^{-2}-\frac{3}{x^2+9}dx \;+\;\int_{3}^{-3}\frac{-2}{4+y^2}dy \;+\;\int_{-2}^{2}-\frac{-3}{x^2+9}dx \;+\; \int_{-3}^{3}\frac{2}{4+y^2}dy \\& =\int_{-2}^{2}\frac{3}{x^2+9}dx \;+\;\int_{-3}^{3}\frac{2}{4+y^2}dy \;+\;\int_{-2}^{2}\frac{3}{x^2+9}dx \;+\; \int_{-3}^{3}\frac{2}{4+y^2}dy \\& =2 \Big(\int_{-2}^{2}\frac{3}{x^2+9}dx \;+\;\int_{-3}^{3}\frac{2}{4+y^2}dy \Big) \\& =4 \Big(\int_{0}^{2}\frac{3}{x^2+9}dx \;+\;\int_{0}^{3}\frac{2}{4+y^2}dy \Big) \\& =4 \Big(\frac{1}{3} \int_{0}^{2} \frac{1} {(\frac{x}{3})^2+1}dx + \frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{3}\frac{1}{1+(\frac{y}{2})^2}dy\Big) \\& =4 \Big(\int_{0}^{2}\frac{1}{(\frac{x}{3})^2+1}d\Big(\frac{x}{3}\Big) + \int_{0}^{3}\frac{1}{1+(\frac{y}{2})^2}d\Big(\frac{y}{2}\Big) \Big) \\& =4\Big(\arctan\Big(\frac{x}{3}\Big)\Big|_{x=0}^{x=2} + \arctan\Big(\frac{y}{2}\Big)\Big|_{y=0}^{y=3}\Big) \\& =4 \Big(\arctan\Big(\frac{2}{3}\Big) + \arctan\Big(\frac{3}{2}\Big)\Big) \\& =4 \Big(\frac{\pi}{2}\Big)=2{\pi}\end{aligned}$$Idea 2 & 3 The corresponding domain $D$ enclosed by $C$ is given as below: Next, $$\int_C\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}dy-\frac{y}{x^2+y^2}dx=\int_C\begin{pmatrix}\frac{-y}{x^2+y^2}\\\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}\end{pmatrix}\cdot\begin{pmatrix}dx\\dy\end{pmatrix}=\int_C F \cdot dr,$$where $F=\begin{pmatrix}\frac{-y}{x^2+y^2}\\ \frac{x}{x^2+y^2}\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}P\\ Q\end{pmatrix}$, $dr=\begin{pmatrix} dx\ dy\end{pmatrix}$, and $r=r(t)$ is the parametric equation of the curve $C$. Note that $F$ is undefined at $(x,y)=(0,0)$.
title: Similar example for Discussion of Green's Theorem (4)

content: 2.2 Idea 2:Assume $ F=\begin{pmatrix}P\\ Q\end{pmatrix}$. We know $F$ is undefined at $O(0,0)$, and then it‚Äôs easy to check $P_y=Q_x$ when $(x,y)$ is on the domain $D$ except the origin $O(0,0)$. Thus, by the test for the conservative vector field, $F$ is conservative on $D$ except $(0,0)$, NOT on the whole region $D$ or for the each point of region $D$. Hence, Fundamental Theorem of Line integrals is NOT applicable here, which is, we can not perform $$\int_C F \cdot dr = f(r(b))-f(r(a)).$$
title: Similar example for Discussion of Green's Theorem (4)

content: 2.3 Idea 3:As $F$ is undefined at $O(0,0)$,the actual domain of integration in this problem should be $$D^*=\{(x,y): D, \text{and } (x,y) \neq (0,0)\}, $$which is a rectangular region with a hole,and we call it non-closed. Hence, Green‚Äôs Theorem can NOT be applied directly to solve this kind of problem. So what else shall we do? We can draw a unit circle, $i.e. C_2:x^2+y^2=1$, where the little hole $O(0,0)$ is within this circle, as shown in the following diagram: Where R is is the region between $C$ and $C_1$, and the boundary of $R$ is $\partial R=C-C_1$ By the Green‚Äôs Theorem, $$\oint_{\partial R} F \cdot dr =\iint_R (Q_x-P_y)dx =0, $$as $P_y=Q_x$ is conservative on the region $R$. $\implies \int_{C_-C_1} F \cdot dr =0$, $\implies \int_{C}F \cdot dr =\int_{C_1}F \cdot dr=2\pi$,based on the result obtained in the remark below . 2.3.1 Remark:(1) Recall, for a vector field $F$, the line integral of $F$ along $C$ is $$\int_C F \cdot dr =\int_a^b F(r(t)) \cdot r'(t)\, dt.$$For $C_1$ in this problem, $r(t)=\begin{pmatrix}\cos t\\ \sin t\end{pmatrix}, 0 \leq t \leq 2\pi$, and hence, $$\int_C\frac{xdy-ydx}{(x^2+y^2)}=\int_C F \cdot dr =\int_0^{2\pi} \begin{pmatrix} -\sin t\\\ \cos t\end{pmatrix} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} -\sin t\\\ \cos t\end{pmatrix}\,dt =2 \pi,$$(2) One of the requirement for Green‚Äôs Theorem is the domain must be closed, for example,

content: 1 QuestionThe temperature at a point $(x,y)$ on a metallic floor is $T(x,y)=4x^2-4xy+y^2$. Wearing thermocouples shoes that measures temperature, you walk along a circle of radius $5$ centered at the origin on the metallic floor. What are the highest and lowest temperatures measured by your shoes?
title: One Exercise about Lagrange Multiplier

content: 2 Solution
title: One Exercise about Lagrange Multiplier

content: 2.1 Lagrange MultiplierThe target: $T(x,y)=4x^2-4xy+y^2\Rightarrow T_x=8x-4y, T_y=-4x+2y$. The constraint: $g(x,y)=x^2+y^2-25=0\Rightarrow g_x=2x, g_y=2y$. By the method of Lagrange Multiplier, {8x‚àí4y=Œª‚ãÖ2x¬Ý‚àí4x+2y=Œª‚ãÖ2y¬Ýx2+y2=25‚áí{4x‚àí2y=Œª‚ãÖx(1)¬Ý4x‚àí2y=Œª‚ãÖ(‚àí2y)(2)¬Ýx2+y2=25(3) \begin{cases} 8x-4y=\lambda\cdot 2x\\\ -4x+2y=\lambda\cdot 2y\\\ x^2+y^2=25 \end{cases} \Rightarrow \begin{cases} 4x-2y=\lambda\cdot x\quad (1)\\\ 4x-2y=\lambda\cdot (-2y)\quad (2)\\\ x^2+y^2=25 \quad (3) \end{cases} ‚é©‚é®‚éß‚Äã8x‚àí4y=Œª‚ãÖ2x¬Ý‚àí4x+2y=Œª‚ãÖ2y¬Ýx2+y2=25‚Äã‚áí‚é©‚é®‚éß‚Äã4x‚àí2y=Œª‚ãÖx(1)¬Ý4x‚àí2y=Œª‚ãÖ(‚àí2y)(2)¬Ýx2+y2=25(3)‚Äã Then, (1)+(2)‚áíŒª‚ãÖx=Œª‚ãÖ(‚àí2y)‚áíŒª‚ãÖ(x‚àí2y)=0‚áíŒª=0¬Ýor¬Ýx=‚àí2y. (1)+(2)\Rightarrow \lambda\cdot x=\lambda\cdot (-2y)\Rightarrow \lambda\cdot (x-2y)=0\Rightarrow \lambda=0\text{ or } x=-2y. (1)+(2)‚áíŒª‚ãÖx=Œª‚ãÖ(‚àí2y)‚áíŒª‚ãÖ(x‚àí2y)=0‚áíŒª=0¬Ýor¬Ýx=‚àí2y. Sub $\lambda=0$ into (1), $4x-2y=0 \Rightarrow y=2x$. Putting them together, $x=-2y$ or $y=2x$. 2.1.1 Method 1Case 1. Sub $x=-2y$ into $(3) \Rightarrow (-2y)^2+y^2=25 \Rightarrow 5y^2=25\Rightarrow y^2=5$. {x=‚àí25¬Ýy=5or{x=25¬Ýy=‚àí5 \begin{cases} x=-2\sqrt{5}\\\ y=\sqrt{5} \end{cases} \quad \text{or} \quad \begin{cases} x=2\sqrt{5}\\\ y=-\sqrt{5} \end{cases} {x=‚àí25‚Äã¬Ýy=5‚Äã‚Äãor{x=25‚Äã¬Ýy=‚àí5‚Äã‚Äãcase 2. Sub $y=2x$ into $(3) \Rightarrow x^{2}+(2 x)^{2}=25 \Rightarrow 5 x^{2}=25 \Rightarrow x^{2}=5$. {x=5¬Ýy=25or{x=‚àí5¬Ýy=‚àí25 \begin{cases} x=\sqrt{5}\\\ y=2\sqrt{5} \end{cases} \quad \text{or} \quad \begin{cases} x=-\sqrt{5}\\\ y=-2\sqrt{5} \end{cases} {x=5‚Äã¬Ýy=25‚Äã‚Äãor{x=‚àí5‚Äã¬Ýy=‚àí25‚Äã‚Äã (1) Sub $x=-2 \sqrt{5}, y=\sqrt{5}$ into $T(x, y)$, T(x,y)=4x2‚àí4xy+y2=(2x‚àíy)2=(‚àí45‚àí5)2=(‚àí55)2=25√ó5=125. T(x, y)=4 x^{2}-4 x y+y^{2}=(2 x-y)^{2}=(-4 \sqrt{5}-\sqrt{5})^{2}=(-5 \sqrt{5})^{2}=25 \times 5=125. T(x,y)=4x2‚àí4xy+y2=(2x‚àíy)2=(‚àí45‚Äã‚àí5‚Äã)2=(‚àí55‚Äã)2=25√ó5=125. (2) Sub $x=2 \sqrt{5}, y=-\sqrt{5}$ into $T(x, y)$, T(x,y)=(2x‚àíy)2=(45+5)2=(55)2=125. T(x, y)=(2 x-y)^{2}=(4 \sqrt{5}+\sqrt{5})^{2}=(5 \sqrt{5})^{2}=125. content: 2.2 Parametric equationsThe target: $T(x, y)=4 x^{2}-4 x y+y^{2} \quad (1)$. The constraint: $g(x, y)=x^{2}+y^{2}-25=0 \quad (2)$. The parametric equation for (2) is {x=5cos‚Å°Œ∏¬Ýy=5sin‚Å°Œ∏ \begin{cases} x=5 \cos \theta\\\ y=5 \sin \theta \end{cases} {x=5cosŒ∏¬Ýy=5sinŒ∏‚Äã and then sub into $(1)$, T(x,y)=(2x‚àíy)2=(10cos‚Å°Œ∏‚àí5sin‚Å°Œ∏)2¬Ý=(5‚ãÖ(2cos‚Å°Œ∏‚àísin‚Å°Œ∏))2¬Ý=25‚ãÖ(2cos‚Å°Œ∏‚àísin‚Å°Œ∏)2¬Ý=25‚ãÖ(5‚ãÖcos‚Å°(Œ∏+Œ±))2¬Ý=25‚ãÖ5‚ãÖcos‚Å°2(Œ∏+Œ±)¬Ý=125‚ãÖcos‚Å°2(Œ∏+Œ±). \begin{aligned} T(x, y)=(2 x-y)^{2} &=(10 \cos \theta-5 \sin \theta)^{2} \\\ &=(5 \cdot(2 \cos \theta-\sin \theta))^{2}\\\ &=25 \cdot(2 \cos \theta-\sin \theta)^{2} \\\ &=25 \cdot(\sqrt{5} \cdot \cos (\theta+\alpha))^{2} \\\ &=25 \cdot 5 \cdot \cos ^{2}(\theta+\alpha)\\\ &=125 \cdot \cos ^{2}(\theta+\alpha). \end{aligned} T(x,y)=(2x‚àíy)2¬Ý‚Äã=(10cosŒ∏‚àí5sinŒ∏)2=(5‚ãÖ(2cosŒ∏‚àísinŒ∏))2=25‚ãÖ(2cosŒ∏‚àísinŒ∏)2=25‚ãÖ(5‚Äã‚ãÖcos(Œ∏+Œ±))2=25‚ãÖ5‚ãÖcos2(Œ∏+Œ±)=125‚ãÖcos2(Œ∏+Œ±).‚ÄãNote that $A\cos\theta-B\sin\theta=\sqrt{A^2+B^2}\cdot \cos (\theta+\alpha)$, where $\alpha=\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{B}{A}\right)$. Hence $2\cos\theta-\sin\theta=\sqrt{5}\cdot\cos(\theta+\alpha)$, $\alpha=\tan^{-1}(\frac{1}{2})$ in line $4$. Note also $-1 \leqslant \cos (\theta+\alpha) \leqslant 1 \Rightarrow 0 \leqslant \cos ^{2}(\theta+\alpha) \leqslant 1 \Rightarrow 0 \leqslant T(x, y) \leqslant 125$. Hence, the highest temperature is $125$, and the lowest one is $0$.
